did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning algorithm based on U-Net (specifically improving sub-modules like Path direct connection, Conv direct connection, Dropout direct connection, and Constant scale) to perform segmentation of skin lesions in melanoma detection using the ISIC 2018 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors improve the U-Net model by proposing four new sub-module structures (Path direct connection, Dropout direct connection, Conv direct connection, and Constant scale). It details the implementation using PyTorch, describes the dataset (ISIC 2018), defines specific evaluation metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, etc.), and reports quantitative results comparing the proposed models against the baseline U-Net. Although published in a conference proceedings (WCSP), it contains original algorithmic development and validation results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: U-Net-based medical image segmentation algorithm
journal: 2021 13th International Conference on Wireless Communications and Signal Processing (WCSP)
brief_summary: This study addresses the segmentation of skin lesions in melanoma detection by improving the U-Net architecture with four new sub-modules: Path direct connection, Dropout direct connection, Conv direct connection, and Constant scale. The authors utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains over 13,000 images from leading international clinical centers, and applied data augmentation techniques such as random rotation and clipping. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F-measure, IOU, Dice coefficient, and a comprehensive score. Experimental results demonstrated that the Constant scale model achieved the highest comprehensive score of 1.2446, outperforming the baseline U-Net and other modified variants. The findings confirm the effectiveness of the proposed structural improvements for medical image segmentation tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the segmentation of skin lesions in melanoma detection using the 2018 ISIC challenge dataset. The task is framed as distinguishing lesion areas (positive) from healthy/background areas (negative) within melanoma images.

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise segmentation mask of skin lesions in melanoma detection images
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'medical image segmentation' using a U-Net based algorithm. The model predicts a binary mask distinguishing lesion pixels (positive) from background/healthy pixels (negative). Evaluation metrics include IOU (Intersection over Union) and Dice coefficient, which are standard for segmentation tasks. The dataset used is the ISIC 2018 challenge dataset for skin lesion segmentation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset (Skin Lesions of Melanoma Detection)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the 2018 ISIC challenge containing images of skin lesions for melanoma detection. The dataset includes over 13,000 images from leading international clinical centers obtained from various devices.

use.training: Used for training the U-Net based models (60% of the dataset).
use.validation: Used for model verification/optimization (20% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation (20% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F-measure, IOU, Dice coefficient, and a comprehensive score) and compared via direct numerical comparison in Table I without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset (Skin Lesion.notes: The Constant scale model achieved the highest comprehensive score (1.2446), ranking first among all experimental models. The U-Net baseline ranked second with a score of 1.2201. The Dropout direct connection model ranked last with a score of 1.1197. The comprehensive score is defined as the sum of IOU and Dice coefficient.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net (specifically variants based on R2U-Net structure)

architecture_details.base_structure: U-Net with contracting path (encoder) and expansive path (decoder), composed of 23 convolutional layers in the baseline.
backbone_components: Residual Network (ResNet) for gradient vanishing prevention via identity mapping (Path direct connection).
Recurrent Convolutional Network (RNN) / Cyclic Convolution Layer (RCL) for feature extraction with memory.
1x1 Convolution for channel interaction and dimension adjustment (Conv direct connection).
Dropout layer for regularization (Dropout direct connection).
Scale weights (a and b) for feature ratio control (Constant scale).
variants_implemented: Path direct connection (R2U-Net baseline): Combines residual network and cyclic convolution network.
Conv direct connection: Adds 1x1 convolution to the input before adding to the cyclic convolution output.
Dropout direct connection: Adds a Dropout layer to the input to prevent overfitting.
Constant scale: Adds scale weights a and b to the converging paths (a=0.5, b=0.5 used in experiments).

training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotation and clipping with 50% probability during data loading.
training_details.hardware: Intel Xeon Silver 4114 CPU @ 2.20GHz, NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1660 (4000Mb VRAM)

classification_or_output_head.description: Pixel-wise binary mask distinguishing lesion pixels (positive) from background/healthy pixels (negative).

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation during data loading: random rotation of melanoma and actual segmentation images by 10° to 350°, random clipping of 0 to 20 pixels around the image, and random horizontal or vertical flipping with 50% probability.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to melanoma and actual segmentation images with a rotation range of 10° to 350°.
Applied 0 to 20 pixels around the image.
Horizontal or vertical flipping with 50% probability.

conclusions: The Constant scale model achieved the best performance among all experimental models in the segmentation task of melanoma skin disease images, with a comprehensive score of 1.2446.
The Constant scale model outperformed the baseline U-Net model (score 1.2201) and other modified variants (Path direct connection, Conv direct connection, and Dropout direct connection).
The proposed structural improvements to the U-Net architecture, specifically the addition of scale weights to control feature ratios, effectively enhanced segmentation performance.
The study confirms that modifying U-Net sub-modules can address specific pain points in medical image segmentation, such as feature extraction and overfitting, using the ISIC 2018 dataset.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (ISIC 2018) without external validation, limiting generalizability to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; model comparisons are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The 'Comprehensive Score' used to rank models is a non-standard metric defined as the sum of IOU and Dice coefficient, which may obscure trade-offs between precision and recall.

The Constant scale model achieved the highest comprehensive score (1.2446) but had lower sensitivity (0.674) compared to the baseline U-Net (0.7195), indicating a potential trade-off in detecting positive lesion cases.
The paper does not report the number of trainable parameters or computational complexity (FLOPs), making it difficult to assess the efficiency gains of the proposed modifications.
Data augmentation was limited to random rotation, clipping, and flipping; more advanced techniques (e.g., elastic deformations, color jittering) were not employed.
The hardware environment (GTX 1660) and training duration (30 epochs) are relatively modest, which may not reflect performance on larger-scale or more complex datasets.
